The seL4 whitepaper is blunt that the fix is structurally unavailable in Linux: POLA capability access control 'is a core design principle of highly secure system, and is impossible to achieve with the way access control happens in mainstream systems such as Linux or Windows' (sel4wp.pdf S1). It also warns that 'Linux capabilities' are a false friend: 'what Linux calls capabilities ... are really access-control lists (ACLs) with system-call granularity. Linux capabilities, like all ACL schemes, suffer from the confused deputy problem, which is at the root of many security breaches' (sel4wp.pdf S4.1). TABOS's stated 'zero ambient authority' model is therefore not novel-to-us (EROS/Capsicum/seL4 own the idea) but is a deliberate, sourced rejection of the Linux/Windows ACL default. Honest silicon note: nothing in x86_64/aarch64 forces ambient authority; the MMU/ring mechanism is policy-neutral.
sources: Watson, Anderson, Laurie, Kennaway, 'Capsicum: practical capabilities for UNIX', USENIX Security 2010 (capsicum.pdf, fetched usenix.org/legacy/event/sec10) - 'ambient authority' definition
seL4 whitepaper (sel4.systems/About/seL4-whitepaper.pdf) S1 'impossible to achieve' in Linux; S4.1 Linux capabilities = ACLs + confused deputy
Capsicum cites EROS [23] / PSOS [16] as the capability provenance for unforgeable tokens of authority

decisions.decision: Do not implement fork() (nor Linux clone()). Tasks are declared statically in a manifest and started by a spawn-from-manifest mechanism (Hubris app.toml model); process/component creation takes explicit parameters, never 'copy everything from the parent and selectively unshare'.
decisions.rationale: The OS-research consensus now treats fork as a mistake, and its recommendation is literally to remove it. Baumann et al.: fork 'was a clever hack ... that has long outlived its usefulness and is now a liability', it is 'a terrible abstraction', 'not thread-safe, it is inefficient and unscalable, and it introduces security concerns', it is 'hostile to user-mode implementation' and 'incompatible with a single address space' / 'heterogeneous hardware'; their explicit call to action is 'Deprecate fork' (fork-hotos19.pdf). Crucially for our security model: 'By default, a forked child inherits everything from its parent ... the inherit-by-default behaviour of fork violates the principle of least [privilege]' - the exact opposite of our capability stance. Windows CreateProcess and posix_spawn() prove process creation needs no fork. Hubris demonstrates the clean-slate alternative working on real silicon: 'Tasks can't be created or destroyed. An application declares all of its tasks in its app.toml config file ... Hubris does not support creating new tasks at runtime ... Put another way, you can't write a fork-bomb without fork' (Hubris Reference S2.4), which also makes peak memory checkable at compile time. fork is software convention, never silicon.
sources: Baumann, Appavoo, Krieger, Roscoe, 'A fork() in the road', HotOS 2019 (fork-hotos19.pdf, fetched microsoft.com/research) - 'liability', 'terrible abstraction', 'violates the principle of least privilege', 'Deprecate fork'
Oxide Hubris Reference S2.4 'Tasks can't be created or destroyed' / 'you can't write a fork-bomb without fork' / app.toml manifest

decisions.decision: No ioctl-style untyped escape hatch and no text-parsing /proc. EVERY operation on a kernel object is a typed capability invocation with a declared method + typed args; all introspection is structured, typed, capability-gated, and model-readable. There is no general 'do-arbitrary-thing-to-this-fd' call and no stringly-typed status surface.
decisions.rationale: seL4 shows the typed alternative is the whole point of a capability kernel: 'invoking a capability is the one and only way of performing an operation on a system object. In fact, a system call in seL4 is a capability invocation, with arguments specifying what operation to perform on the object referenced by the invoked capability. The kernel will then check whether the capability authorises the requested operation, and immediately abort ... if it is not authorised' - 'this fine-grained, object-oriented nature' is precisely what ioctl's opaque (fd, request, void*) lacks (sel4wp.pdf S4.1). Capsicum independently flags ioctl as inherently dangerous: among 'system management interfaces that must be protected' are '/dev device nodes ... and some ioctl operations on sockets' (capsicum.pdf S2.1) - i.e. ioctl is an unbounded authority surface even in a hardened Unix. The /proc text surface is the same untyped fragility on the read side; replacing it with typed introspection also directly serves the LLM-agnostic / 'structured model-readable' goal. None of this is silicon-mandated - ioctl and /proc are pure Unix convention.
sources: seL4 whitepaper (sel4.systems) S4.1 - 'a system call in seL4 is a capability invocation ... kernel will then check whether the capability authorises the requested operation'
Capsicum (capsicum.pdf) S2.1 - 'some ioctl operations on sockets' listed among management interfaces that must be protected to maintain isolation

decisions.decision: No POSIX signals (no async signal handlers, no async-signal-safety constraint, no signal masks). Asynchronous events are delivered as typed, queued notifications to an explicit endpoint that a task drains synchronously at safe points (Hubris-style notification bits / IPC), never as a forced reentrant jump into handler context.
decisions.rationale: The Linux man-pages themselves document signals as a structurally broken async primitive: 'An async-signal-safe function is one that can be safely called from within a signal handler. Many functions are not async-signal-safe. In particular, nonreentrant functions are generally unsafe' - and the worked example shows a printf interrupted mid-buffer by a handler that also calls printf yields 'inconsistent data, with unpredictable results' (signal-safety(7), Linux man-pages 6.18). The standard's 'solutions' (call only the ~180 whitelisted functions, or block signals around every critical section) are conceded to be impractical: 'the second choice is difficult in programs of any complexity'. Even errno is fragile: it is only async-signal-safe 'provided that the signal handler saves errno on entry and restores its value before returning'. A clean-slate kernel inherits none of this: signals are a 1970s convention, not silicon. Hubris's notification model (typed bits drained on the task's own schedule) is the structural replacement we adopt.
sources: Linux man-pages 6.18, signal-safety(7) (man7.org/linux/man-pages/man7/signal-safety.7.html) - 'Many functions are not async-signal-safe'; printf reentrancy example; errno save/restore caveat
Oxide Hubris Reference - notification/IPC delivery model (typed, drained synchronously) as the non-signal async primitive

decisions.decision: No path-based ambient access; close the TOCTOU class by construction. There is no global filesystem/PID namespace a task can name. Access is via handles only; designation and authority are coupled in one unforgeable capability ('no designation without authority'). A task can only operate on objects it already holds a capability for - there is no name-it-then-check-it window.
decisions.rationale: TOCTOU is a direct consequence of separating naming (a path, available to anyone) from authorization (a later check), which Unix's path-based API bakes in. seL4 states the fix as a principle: 'denomination (the reference to the file) and authority (the right to perform operations on the file) must be coupled, a principle called no designation without authority ... This is exactly what a capability system enforces' (sel4wp.pdf S4.1) - removing the confused-deputy / race surface entirely. Capsicum demonstrates the same in practice: capability-mode processes 'are denied access to global namespaces such as the filesystem and PID namespaces'; openat against a directory capability means 'openat(4, "../etc/passwd") and openat(4, "/etc/passwd") will not [succeed]', and the paper explicitly discusses how lifting the '..' restriction would reintroduce a race where 'two colluding sandboxes ... can race to actively rearrange a tree so that the check always succeeds' - i.e. classic TOCTOU, which the handle-relative design avoids (capsicum.pdf S2.1-2.2). Path-based access is Unix convention; silicon imposes nothing here.
sources: seL4 whitepaper S4.1 - 'no designation without authority'; designation+authority must be coupled
Capsicum (capsicum.pdf) S2.1/S2.2 - capability mode denies global namespaces; openat refuses absolute and '..' paths; explicit TOCTOU race discussion ('race to actively rearrange a tree')

decisions.decision: No global integer file-descriptor table and no forgeable/guessable references. Object references are unforgeable capability handles indexed in a per-task capability space (CSpace), each carrying a narrow rights mask; there is no ambient 'fd 0/1/2 are stdin/out/err' inheritance and no integer-exhaustion or fd-confusion attack surface.
decisions.rationale: Capsicum observes that Unix fds are already half-capabilities but dangerously over-broad: 'File descriptors already have some properties of capabilities: they are unforgeable tokens of authority ... Unlike pure capabilities, however, they confer very broad rights: even if a file descriptor is read-only, operations on meta-data such as fchmod are permitted' - which is exactly why Capsicum adds a ~60-bit rights mask (CAP_READ, CAP_SEEK, ...) per descriptor (capsicum.pdf S2.2). seL4 generalizes: a capability 'is an object reference; in that sense it is similar to a pointer ... fat pointers ... immutable pointers ... each capability uniquely specifies a particular object' plus an encoded rights set (sel4wp.pdf S4.1). TABOS adopts the strong form: unforgeable handles in a CSpace with minimal rights, not small integers. The integer-fd model (small dense namespace, ambient 0/1/2, EMFILE exhaustion, fd reuse races) is Unix convention, not silicon - the CPU has no notion of a file descriptor.
sources: Capsicum (capsicum.pdf) S2.2 - fds are 'unforgeable tokens of authority' but 'confer very broad rights' (fchmod on read-only fd); ~60 rights-mask bits per capability
seL4 whitepaper S4.1 - capability as immutable 'fat pointer' (object reference + access rights) uniquely specifying an object

decisions.decision: No C-string / errno ABI at any TABOS boundary. The kernel ABI is Rust-native: fallible operations return typed Result values with structured, enumerable, model-readable error variants; there is no thread-local errno, no NUL-terminated string contract, and no out-of-band -1/set-errno convention. (tb-hal confines the only unsafe/asm; the ABI above it is total and typed.)
decisions.rationale: This is honestly a TABOS ABI choice rather than a single named paper-critique, but it is grounded: even POSIX concedes errno is fragile state - 'Fetching and setting the value of errno is async-signal-safe provided that the signal handler saves errno on entry and restores its value before returning' (signal-safety(7)), i.e. correctness depends on every caller's discipline. The errno/-1 convention and NUL-terminated C strings are conventions, not silicon, and they are exactly the legacy a Rust-frozen-capability microkernel exists to delete (typed Result<T,E>, slices with length, no ambient global). Choosing typed structured errors also directly serves the LLM-agnostic 'model-readable' requirement: an LLM can pattern-match an enum variant far more reliably than parse errno integers or English strerror strings. We own this surface; we do not inherit it.
sources: Linux man-pages 6.18, signal-safety(7) - errno is only safe with manual save/restore discipline (evidence the errno convention is brittle shared state)
TABOS scenario - Rust frozen capability microkernel; tb-hal isolates unsafe/asm; ABI above it is typed (own-structure decision, not a Linux inheritance)

decisions.decision: Do not adopt the synchronous-blocking syscall model as the only/primary contract. The default is async capability-invocation; long-running LLM/inference work is submitted as a DAG of typed requests and completed asynchronously (memory-first, self-improvement). Blocking is an opt-in convenience built on top, never the substrate.
decisions.rationale: The async/DAG inference-submission structure is genuinely TABOS-novel (it is our own design for the LLM-agnostic, memory-first, single-vCPU Mirage scenario), so I classify it tabos-novel rather than pretend a paper mandates it. Honesty about silicon: the syscall TRAP instruction (x86_64 SYSCALL, aarch64 SVC) and the privilege-level transition ARE silicon-unavoidable - we cannot avoid the instruction set. But the BLOCKING SEMANTICS layered on the trap are pure software convention; Unix chose synchronous-by-default and bolted on epoll/io_uring/AIO afterward. A clean-slate kernel can make async the native shape from day one. Supporting evidence that fork-era synchronous assumptions are exactly what modern designs discard: the fork paper notes 'clean-slate designs' that 'can manipulate any process ... rather than [only] the current process', yielding flexibility the synchronous fork/exec line lacks (fork-hotos19.pdf S6). We own the inference-DAG contract; the trap instruction underneath it we honestly cannot.
sources: TABOS scenario - LLM-agnostic, memory-first, self-improvement; DAG/async inference submission is TABOS's own structure

Baumann et al., 'A fork() in the road', HotOS 2019 (fork-hotos19.pdf) S6 - clean-slate designs decouple operations from 'the current process' (evidence the synchronous-self model is a discardable convention)
Silicon boundary (to verify against Intel SDM Vol.2 SYSCALL / ARM ARM SVC): the trap instruction + ring transition are mandated; the blocking semantics on top are not

hardFacts.fact: fork() is a Unix/POSIX software API, not a CPU instruction. Windows CreateProcess and POSIX posix_spawn() create processes with no fork, and the HotOS'19 authors' explicit recommendation is 'Deprecate fork'. => Process creation semantics are 100% choosable; rejecting fork costs us no silicon compatibility.
hardFacts.source: fork-hotos19.pdf (Baumann et al., HotOS 2019) - 'Deprecate fork'; Windows CreateProcess / posix_spawn cited as fork-free

hardFacts.fact: The seL4 whitepaper states that principle-of-least-authority capability access control 'is impossible to achieve with the way access control happens in mainstream systems such as Linux or Windows.' => The capability model is structurally NON-Linux; adopting it is a clean break, not a port of a Linux feature.

What Linux calls 'capabilities' (CAP_NET_ADMIN etc.) 'are really access-control lists (ACLs) with system-call granularity' and 'suffer from the confused deputy problem.' => Object-capabilities (seL4/EROS/Capsicum style) are a DIFFERENT mechanism; we are not inheriting or renaming Linux capabilities.

A Unix file descriptor is 'an unforgeable token of authority' but 'confer[s] very broad rights' (e.g. fchmod works even on a read-only fd), which is why Capsicum bolts a ~60-bit rights mask onto each one. => The integer-fd model is a half-capability with ambient over-broad authority; TABOS's narrow-rights CSpace handles are the corrective, and integer fds are not silicon.

The Linux man-pages themselves document POSIX signals as structurally unsafe: 'Many functions are not async-signal-safe ... nonreentrant functions are generally unsafe to call from a signal handler,' and blocking-signals-everywhere is 'difficult in programs of any complexity.' => Signals are a discardable broken async primitive, not a hardware-imposed mechanism.

Capsicum lists 'some ioctl operations on sockets' among the dangerous 'system management interfaces that must be protected,' and seL4 makes every operation a typed, authorized capability invocation instead. => ioctl is an untyped authority escape hatch (Unix convention), replaceable by typed cap-invocations with no silicon cost.
hardFacts.source: capsicum.pdf S2.1 (ioctl as protected mgmt interface); seL4 whitepaper S4.1 ('a system call in seL4 is a capability invocation')

hardFacts.fact: TOCTOU arises from Unix separating designation (a path anyone can name) from authority (a later check); seL4's 'no designation without authority' and Capsicum's openat-relative-to-directory-capability (refusing absolute and '..' paths) eliminate the race by construction. => Path-based ambient access is convention; handle/capability access closes the TOCTOU class without hardware support.
hardFacts.source: seL4 whitepaper S4.1 ('no designation without authority'); capsicum.pdf S2.2 (openat refuses '/etc/passwd' and '../etc/passwd'; explicit tree-rearrange race)

hardFacts.fact: Hubris (Oxide's shipping Rust microkernel) runs on real silicon with NO runtime task creation: all tasks are declared in app.toml, so 'you can't write a fork-bomb without fork,' and peak memory is checkable at compile time. => The static spawn-from-manifest model is field-proven, not theoretical.
hardFacts.source: Oxide Hubris Reference, Section 2.4 'Tasks can't be created or destroyed'
